title: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization
rawPassageText:
READING PASSAGE 2
You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14-26, which are based on Reading Passage 2 below.

Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

A

Political disagreement existed long before social media, but online platforms have changed the speed and scale at which disagreement travels. A post that provokes anger, fear or pride may be shared faster than a calm explanation, partly because emotion gives users a reason to react immediately. Algorithms do not create those emotions from nothing. They organise, rank and recommend material in ways that can make emotional posts more visible. This matters because visibility is not neutral: when millions of people repeatedly see the most forceful versions of an argument, they may begin to mistake intensity for importance.

B

Most large platforms use ranking systems that predict engagement. These systems learn from signals such as clicks, watch time, comments and shares. If a user often pauses over videos about a particular political topic, the system may offer more of the same. The algorithm does not need to understand the full meaning of a post in order to promote it; it only needs to predict that the post will keep someone active on the platform. Over time, this can create feedback loops in which user behaviour shapes recommendations, and recommendations then shape future behaviour.

C

Researchers disagree about how strong the link is between algorithms and polarization. Some studies find that recommendation systems can push users towards more extreme material, especially when platforms reward long viewing sessions. Other studies suggest that people often bring strong political identities with them before they open an app. The effect also depends on platform design, local politics and user habits. For this reason, it is misleading to claim that algorithms are the single cause of polarization. They are better understood as amplifiers that interact with existing divisions.

D

One problem is that different groups can see the same event through different streams of information. During an election dispute, for example, one set of users may repeatedly receive posts describing the event as evidence of corruption, while another set may see posts presenting the same event as a normal legal disagreement. Neither group necessarily realises how different the other information stream is. The result is not only disagreement about opinions, but disagreement about what has happened. In such conditions, public debate becomes harder because people no longer share the same starting facts.

E

Several experiments have tested what happens when users see posts in chronological order rather than through algorithmic ranking. Chronological feeds can reduce exposure to some highly partisan material, because posts are no longer selected mainly for predicted engagement. However, the results are mixed. Some users spend less time on the platform when ranking is removed, while others seek out political content by following particular accounts or joining groups. In addition, short experiments may not be long enough to change attitudes that have developed over years. The design of a feed matters, but it is not a magic switch.

F

Platforms have tried different interventions. Fact-checking labels can warn users before they share a disputed claim, and prompts can ask people whether they have opened an article before reposting it. Some systems reduce the distribution of posts that are repeatedly flagged by independent reviewers. These designs add friction to sharing, slowing down the movement of material that might otherwise travel instantly. Critics argue that such measures can be inconsistent or politically controversial, but supporters say that slowing impulsive sharing is a practical way to reduce the reach of misleading content.

G

Removing algorithms entirely would not necessarily produce a healthier public sphere. Without ranking, users might face an unmanageable flood of posts, advertisements and repeated messages. Some form of selection is needed whenever there is more content than a person can read. The harder question is what the selection should reward. A system designed only for engagement may favour conflict, while a system designed with civic goals might value reliability, diversity of sources and time for reflection. The debate is therefore not about whether platforms should organise information, but about the values built into that organisation.

Questions 14-20

Reading Passage 2 has seven paragraphs, A-G.

Which paragraph contains the following information?

Write the correct letter, A-G, in boxes 14-20 on your answer sheet.

14 mention of disagreement among strength algorithmic influence

15 reference to algorithms using behaviour without fully understanding content

16 reference to increasing speed posts
17 an example of two audiences being shown contrasting readings of one incident

18 reference to comparing feeds

19 examples of platform designs pause spreading

20 an explanation of systems completely may solve problem

Questions 21-24

Complete the summary below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.

Write your answers in boxes 21-24 on your answer sheet.

How recommendation systems may affect debate

Many platforms rank posts by predicting 21 .................... . They learn from signals such as clicks, watch time, comments and 22 .................... . When users continue to interact with similar material, this can create feedback 23 .................... . Some platform designs try to add 24 .................... before users pass on questionable information.

Questions 25 and 26

Choose TWO letters, A-H.

Which TWO statements are true according to the passage?

A Algorithms are described as the only cause of modern political polarization.
B Chronological feeds can reduce exposure to some highly partisan material.
C Fact-checking labels always change users' political identities.
D Algorithms must understand the full meaning of posts before promoting them.
E Users play no part in shaping what recommendation systems show them.
F Some design choices can slow impulsive sharing.
G The writer argues that platforms do not need to organise information.
H All studies agree that algorithms have the same effect in every country.
rawAnswerKey:
Question 14: mention of disagreement among strength algorithmic influence

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: Researchers disagree about how strong the link is between algorithms and polarization.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะย่อหน้า C เริ่มด้วยประโยคว่า Researchers disagree about how strong the link is between algorithms and polarization ซึ่งตรงกับโจทย์ที่ถามเรื่อง disagreement among researchers about the strength of algorithmic influence โดยตรง ย่อหน้านี้ยังอธิบายต่อว่าบางงานวิจัยพบผลแรงกว่า ขณะที่บางงานชี้ว่าผู้ใช้มี political identities มาก่อน

Paraphrased Vocabulary: among strength algorithmic influence = researchers disagree strong link between algorithms polarization; answer signal = C



Question 15: reference to algorithms using behaviour without fully understanding content

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: These systems learn from signals such as clicks, watch time, comments and shares. If a user often pauses over videos about a particular political topic, the system may offer more of the same. The algorithm does not need to understand the full meaning of a post in order to promote it

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะย่อหน้า B อธิบายตรง ๆ ว่า ranking systems เรียนรู้จาก behaviour signals เช่น clicks, watch time, comments และ shares และยังบอกว่า algorithm ไม่จำเป็นต้อง understand the full meaning of a post เพื่อ promote it โจทย์ถามทั้งสองส่วนคือใช้สัญญาณพฤติกรรมและไม่ได้เข้าใจเนื้อหาเต็ม ๆ ซึ่งรวมอยู่ในย่อหน้า B ชัดเจนที่สุด

Paraphrased Vocabulary: without fully understanding content = these systems learn signals such clicks watch time; answer signal = B



Question 16: reference to increasing speed posts

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: A post that provokes anger, fear or pride may be shared faster than a calm explanation, partly because emotion gives users a reason to react immediately.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะย่อหน้า A กล่าวว่าข้อความที่กระตุ้น anger, fear หรือ pride อาจถูก shared faster กว่าคำอธิบายที่สงบ เพราะ emotion ทำให้ผู้ใช้ react immediately โจทย์ถามเรื่อง emotion เพิ่ม speed ของการแชร์ ซึ่งตรงกับ shared faster และ react immediately ในย่อหน้า A

Paraphrased Vocabulary: increasing speed posts = post provokes anger fear pride may shared faster; answer signal = A



Question 17: an example of two audiences being shown contrasting readings of one incident

Correct Answer: D

Accepted Answers: D

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: one set of users may repeatedly receive posts describing the event as evidence of corruption, while another set may see posts presenting the same event as a normal legal disagreement

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ D เพราะย่อหน้า D ยกตัวอย่าง election dispute ที่ผู้ใช้สองกลุ่มเห็นการตีความเหตุการณ์เดียวกันต่างกัน กลุ่มหนึ่งเห็นว่าเป็น evidence of corruption แต่อีกกลุ่มเห็นว่าเป็น normal legal disagreement โจทย์ใช้คำว่า different interpretations of the same event ซึ่งตรงกับ same event ที่ถูกนำเสนอคนละแบบในย่อหน้า D

Paraphrased Vocabulary: two groups = one set of users / another set; different interpretations = corruption / normal legal disagreement; same event = the same event; answer signal = D

Question 18: reference to comparing feeds

Correct Answer: E

Accepted Answers: E

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: Several experiments have tested what happens when users see posts in chronological order rather than through algorithmic ranking.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ E เพราะย่อหน้า E กล่าวถึง experiments โดยตรง และระบุว่า tested what happens when users see posts in chronological order rather than through algorithmic ranking โจทย์ถามการเปรียบเทียบ chronological feeds กับ algorithmic ranking จึงตรงกับย่อหน้า E ชัดเจน

Paraphrased Vocabulary: comparing feeds = several experiments tested happens users see posts chronological; answer signal = E



Question 19: examples of platform designs pause spreading

Correct Answer: F

Accepted Answers: F

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: Fact-checking labels can warn users before they share a disputed claim, and prompts can ask people whether they have opened an article before reposting it.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ F เพราะย่อหน้า F ยกตัวอย่าง interventions เช่น fact-checking labels ที่เตือนก่อน share และ prompts ที่ถามว่าผู้ใช้เปิด article แล้วหรือยัง ก่อน reposting it สิ่งเหล่านี้เป็น platform designs ที่ทำให้ผู้ใช้หยุดคิดหรือ pause ก่อน spreading information ดังนั้น heading information นี้อยู่ในย่อหน้า F

Paraphrased Vocabulary: platform designs pause spreading = fact checking labels warn users before share disputed; answer signal = F



Question 20: an explanation of systems completely may solve problem

Correct Answer: G

Accepted Answers: G

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: Removing algorithms entirely would not necessarily produce a healthier public sphere. Without ranking, users might face an unmanageable flood of posts, advertisements and repeated messages.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ G เพราะย่อหน้า G อธิบายว่า removing algorithms entirely ไม่จำเป็นต้องทำให้ public sphere ดีขึ้น เพราะถ้าไม่มี ranking ผู้ใช้อาจเจอ unmanageable flood of posts, advertisements and repeated messages โจทย์ถามเหตุผลว่าทำไมการเอา ranking systems ออกทั้งหมดอาจไม่แก้ปัญหา ซึ่งย่อหน้า G ตอบตรงที่สุด

Paraphrased Vocabulary: completely may solve problem = removing algorithms entirely necessarily produce healthier public sphere; answer signal = G



Question 21: Many platforms, and rank posts ....................
Correct Answer: engagement

Accepted Answers: engagement

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: Most large platforms use ranking systems that predict engagement.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ engagement เพราะ summary ใช้ถ้อยคำถอดความ ไม่ได้คัดลอกวลีจาก passage ตรงช่องว่าง — summary ถามว่า platforms rank posts by predicting ____ และ passage ระบุว่า use ranking systems that predict engagement โดยตรง คำว่า many platforms ใน summary เทียบกับ most large platforms ได้ และคำตอบต้องเป็นคำเดียวจาก passage คือ engagement


Paraphrased Vocabulary: many platforms and rank posts = most large platforms use ranking systems that predict engagement; answer signal = engagement

Question 22: Answer signal ....................
Correct Answer: shares

Accepted Answers: shares

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: These systems learn from signals such as clicks, watch time, comments and shares.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ shares เพราะ summary ใช้ถ้อยคำถอดความ ไม่ได้คัดลอกวลีจาก passage ตรงช่องว่าง — summary ใช้รายการเดียวกับ passage คือ clicks, watch time, comments and ____ ส่วน passage ระบุเต็มว่า clicks, watch time, comments and shares คำตอบจึงเป็น shares ซึ่งเป็นคำเดียวและมาจาก passage โดยตรง


Paraphrased Vocabulary: answer signal = these systems learn from signals such as clicks watch time; answer signal = shares

Question 23: Continue to interact with similar material, and answer signal ....................
Correct Answer: loops

Accepted Answers: loops

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: this can create feedback loops in which user behaviour shapes recommendations, and recommendations then shape future behaviour.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ loops เพราะ summary ใช้ถ้อยคำถอดความ ไม่ได้คัดลอกวลีจาก passage ตรงช่องว่าง — summary บอกว่า interaction กับ similar material สามารถ create feedback ____ และ passage ระบุว่า this can create feedback loops โดยตรง คำว่า users continue to interact with similar material สรุปจาก user often pauses over topic และ offer more of the same ดังนั้นคำที่เติมคือ loops


Paraphrased Vocabulary: similar material and answer signal = this can create feedback loops in which user behaviour shapes; answer signal = loops

Question 24: Questionable information, and platform designs ....................
Correct Answer: friction

Accepted Answers: friction

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: These designs add friction to sharing, slowing down the movement of material that might otherwise travel instantly.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ friction เพราะ summary ใช้ถ้อยคำถอดความ ไม่ได้คัดลอกวลีจาก passage ตรงช่องว่าง — summary ถามว่า platform designs add ____ before users pass on questionable information และ passage ระบุว่า designs add friction to sharing คำว่า pass on questionable information สรุปจาก sharing disputed claims หรือ misleading content ในย่อหน้าเดียวกัน คำตอบจึงเป็น friction


Paraphrased Vocabulary: questionable information and platform designs = these designs add friction to sharing slowing down the movement; answer signal = friction

Question 25: Which TWO statements are true according to the passage?

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: Chronological feeds can reduce exposure to some highly partisan material

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบหนึ่งคือ B เพราะ passage กล่าวตรง ๆ ว่า Chronological feeds can reduce exposure to some highly partisan material ซึ่งเหมือนตัวเลือก B เกือบทั้งหมด ตัวเลือกนี้จึงเป็น statement ที่ true according to the passage ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นจำนวนมากใช้คำสุดโต่ง เช่น only, always, no part หรือ all studies ซึ่งขัดกับ passage หรือไม่มีข้อมูลรองรับ

Paraphrased Vocabulary: reduce exposure = reduce exposure; highly partisan material = highly partisan material; true statement = directly stated; answer signal = B

Question 26: Which TWO statements are true according to the passage?

Correct Answer: F

Accepted Answers: F

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading April 2026 Passage 2 - Social Media Algorithms and Polarization

Exact Portion: supporters say that slowing impulsive sharing is a practical way to reduce the reach of misleading content.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบอีกข้อคือ F เพราะ passage ระบุว่า designs add friction to sharing และ supporters say that slowing impulsive sharing is a practical way to reduce misleading content ดังนั้นตัวเลือก F ที่บอกว่า design choices can slow impulsive sharing จึงตรงกับ passage ชัดเจน ข้อนี้เป็นหนึ่งในสอง statements ที่ true ตามบทความ

Paraphrased Vocabulary: design choices = designs / interventions; can slow = slowing; impulsive sharing = impulsive sharing; answer signal = F
